{"step_by_step_thinking": "In electrophoresis, lipoproteins migrate toward the charged end based on their net charge and size. However, lipoproteins are generally negatively charged due to their apolipoproteins and phospholipid components. The key factor in electrophoresis is the density and composition of the lipoprotein, which affects its migration.

From the retrieved documents, Document [27] (Biochemistry, Chylomicron -- Introduction) states: 'Chylomicrons have a density below 0.94 g/ml and remain at the origin of lipoprotein electrophoresis.' This indicates that chylomicrons do not migrate toward the charged end; they remain at the starting point because of their very low density and high triglyceride content.

In contrast, other lipoproteins like VLDL, LDL, and HDL have higher densities and do migrate toward the charged end during electrophoresis. HDL, being the most dense, migrates the furthest, while VLDL and LDL migrate in between.

Therefore, chylomicrons are the only lipoprotein among the options that does not move toward the charged end in electrophoresis due to their low density and position at the origin.", "answer_choice": "D"}
